name: Costa da Morte Loop via Vimianzo and Laxe
name_original: Negreira - vuelta al Embalse de Fervenza y Playa de Laxe
description: This difficult 88.3-mile road cycling tour from Negreira to Laxe Beach via Fervenza Reservoir offers diverse Galician landscapes and ocean v
region_intro: Road cycling around Santa Comba offers diverse landscapes, from river valleys to mountainous terrain. The region is characterized by the Dão and Mondego rivers, which carve through picturesque schist and granite formations. Dedicated cycling infrastructure, such as the Ecopista do Dão, provides smooth paths along the Dão River. This area presents a variety of road cycling challenges, including routes with significant elevation gain. Best road cycling routes around Santa Comba The most popular road cycling route is Costa da Morte Cycling Loop, a 79.5 miles (127.9 km) trail that takes 6 hours 16 minutes to complete. This difficult route features substantial elevation gain through varied landscapes. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Costa da Morte Loop via Vimianzo and Laxe, a difficult 88.3 miles (142.1 km) path. This route offers extensive riding with nearly 2,000 meters of ascent, passing through coastal and inland scenery. Local road cyclists also love the Museo Terra do Xallas loop from Esmorode, a 45.1 miles (72.6 km) trail leading through rural areas with significant climbs, often completed in about 3 hours 27 minutes. Road cycling around Santa Comba is defined by river valleys, schist and granite mountains, and the well-maintained Ecopista do Dão. The network offers options for both moderate and difficult ability levels, with 9 moderate and 10 difficult routes available. The routes in Santa Comba are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 2 reviews. More than 90 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Santa Comba's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of road cycling routes can I find around Santa Comba?
A: Santa Comba offers a diverse range of road cycling routes, catering to various skill levels. You'll find paths winding through river valleys, alongside the Dão and Mondego rivers, and ascending through picturesque schist and granite mountain landscapes. The region features both moderate and difficult routes, with 9 moderate and 10 difficult options available.

Q: Are there easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Santa Comba?
A: While the guide primarily highlights moderate to difficult routes, the region does offer accessible options. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/guide/1577027/road-cycling-routes-around-santa-comba" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ecopista do Dão&lt;/a&gt;, though not explicitly listed in the top routes, is a well-regarded, smooth asphalt path that follows the Dão River for 47.5 km, offering a moderate difficulty ride suitable for those looking for a less strenuous experience.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: Experienced cyclists looking for a challenge will find several difficult routes with significant elevation gain. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e968702842/costa-da-morte-loop-via-vimianzo-and-laxe" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Costa da Morte Loop via Vimianzo and Laxe&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 142.1 km path with nearly 2,000 meters of ascent. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e971627508/costa-da-morte-cycling-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Costa da Morte Cycling Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 127.9 km with substantial climbing through varied landscapes.

Q: What scenic viewpoints or landmarks can I expect to see on road cycling routes?
A: The region is rich in natural beauty and viewpoints. Cycling along the Dão River, especially on the Ecopista do Dão, offers beautiful vistas. The Outeirinho Viewpoint provides panoramic views over the Dão River. Further afield, the Livraria do Mondego is a unique rock formation. You might also encounter picturesque windmills in areas like Gavinhos and Serra da Atalhada, offering views over the Serra do Buçaco.

Q: Are there any historical sites or natural attractions along the routes?
A: Yes, Santa Comba Dão and its surroundings blend natural beauty with historical charm. The town itself features historical sites like the Casa dos Arcos and the 18th-century Igreja Matriz. While cycling, you might pass through areas near &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/586080" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Negreira — Historic Town&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/418330" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ferreiro Square Negreira&lt;/a&gt;. Natural features like the Cascatas de Sevilha (waterfalls) are also found in the vicinity.

Q: Are there any long-distance road cycling routes in the Santa Comba area?
A: For long-distance enthusiasts, Santa Comba Dão is a gateway to extensive cycling networks. The iconic National Road 2 (EN2) passes through the interior of Portugal, offering a 717.7 km journey. Additionally, the Grande Rota do Mondego (GR48) spans 142 kilometers, unifying landscapes and culture along the Mondego River.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes around Santa Comba?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes in the area are designed as loops. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6975442" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Museo Terra do Xallas loop from Esmorode&lt;/a&gt;, a 72.6 km route through rural areas, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/1271288" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santa Mariña – Brandomil Bridge loop from Esmorode&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 53.4 km option.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Santa Comba?
A: May is noted as a particularly popular month for cyclists in the region, suggesting favorable weather conditions. Generally, spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures for road cycling, avoiding the peak heat of summer and the cooler, potentially wetter conditions of winter.

Q: Are there options for parking near road cycling routes?
A: While specific parking locations for each route are not detailed, many routes start from towns or villages like Esmorode, where parking facilities are typically available. For routes like the Ecopista do Dão, access points in Santa Comba Dão often have designated parking areas.

Q: How can I access road cycling routes using public transport?
A: Santa Comba Dão is a municipality with public transport connections, which can serve as a starting point for many routes. For instance, the Ecopista do Dão connects municipalities including Viseu, Tondela, and Santa Comba Dão, making it accessible from various points within the region via local transport networks.

Q: Are there places to stop for refreshments or food along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes pass through or near towns and villages where you can find cafes, restaurants, and local shops. Santa Comba Dão itself is a historic town with amenities. For longer routes like the Grande Rota do Mondego, you'll encounter various municipalities that offer opportunities for refreshments and food.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Santa Comba?
A: The road cycling routes in Santa Comba are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, from river valleys to mountainous landscapes, and the well-maintained infrastructure like the Ecopista do Dão. The combination of natural beauty and challenging climbs makes it a rewarding destination.
tips: Laxe beach in Costa da Morte, A Coruña. White sand beach 2 km long and crystal clear waters

XII century castle built by the Mariño de Lobeira family and later by the Moscoso family, one of the most influential in the province. In 1973, sold to the Diputación de A Coruña, the necessary rehabilitation works were undertaken on the castle to dedicate it to public use, and it is currently managed by the Vimianzo Council with free access.
Parish of the town of San Vicenzo
Beautiful town characterized by its musical history since it has its own school where dozens of students pass each year.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this road cycling tour?
faqs.answer: This road cycling tour is considered difficult, requiring good to very good fitness. Galicia's mountainous geography means significant elevation gains, even on routes described as medium. Expect continuous climbs and descents, particularly along the coastal sections.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this tour?
faqs.answer: The tour offers diverse Galician scenery. You'll start in an inland, rural setting around Negreira, passing through lush forests. As you approach the Fervenza Reservoir, expect paved roads winding through hilly landscapes. The transition to Laxe Beach brings dramatic Atlantic coastline views, with coastal roads featuring continuous climbs and descents typical of the Costa da Morte.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: This tour connects several notable locations. You'll pass through Negreira, a town known as a starting point for various routes. The Fervenza Reservoir area offers natural beauty. Further along, you can see Vimianzo Castle and San Vicenzo. The tour culminates at Laxe Beach, part of the wild Costa da Morte.

faqs.question: Where does this road cycling tour typically start and end?
faqs.answer: The tour is designed to start in Negreira, an inland town that serves as a common departure point for routes in the region. It then progresses towards the Fervenza Reservoir and concludes at Laxe Beach on the coast.

faqs.question: Is there parking available near the starting point or along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, parking is generally available in towns like Negreira, which is a common starting point. Along the route, particularly near the Fervenza Reservoir and Laxe Beach, you can usually find parking options, though these might be more limited in peak season.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to undertake this road cycling tour?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle in Galicia, including this route, is typically during the spring (April-June) and early autumn (September-October). During these periods, the weather is generally milder, and the landscapes are vibrant, offering a more comfortable and scenic experience than the hotter summer months or wetter winter.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this tour?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route. It primarily uses public roads and access points to natural areas.

faqs.question: Are there any specific regulations or fees for cycling within the Costa da Morte region?
faqs.answer: While there are no general entrance fees for the Costa da Morte region itself, cyclists should always adhere to standard road cycling regulations. Be mindful of local traffic laws and respect any specific rules posted in protected natural areas or villages you pass through. Some smaller, unpaved paths might be restricted to hiking or mountain biking.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling tour dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: As a road cycling tour, bringing a dog would typically mean they accompany you in a trailer or at stops. While dogs are generally welcome in public spaces and many natural areas in Galicia, always keep them on a leash, especially near roads or in populated areas. Check local regulations for specific beaches or natural parks if you plan to stop with your dog.

faqs.question: Does the tour intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route overlaps with sections of several other paths. Notably, it covers parts of the Mtb Vía Mariana (10%), Via Mariana (10%), and the Camiño de Fisterra-Muxía (8%), which is a significant part of the Camino de Finisterre. You might also encounter smaller sections of the O Camiño dos Faros: Etapa 3 Ponteceso-Laxe (4%) and Ruta dos Tres Pazos (2%).

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or places to resupply along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, you will find opportunities for cafes and resupply in the towns you pass through, such as Negreira at the start, and certainly in Laxe at the end. Along the route, especially in smaller villages or near the Fervenza Reservoir, options might be less frequent, so it's wise to carry sufficient water and snacks.
